threads: building the Kasett compaction plugin itself — meta-benchmark
designing the thread meta schema and storage format
implementing the session reader for rolling window
designing the parser for THREAD_META blocks

messages.content: Let's build the Kasett compaction plugin. Package: @moltai/kasett-rewind. Source: /home/node/.openclaw/workspace/repos/kasett-rewind/src/index.ts

Building @moltai/kasett-rewind v0.1.0. Entry point: /home/node/.openclaw/workspace/repos/kasett-rewind/src/index.ts. Plugin ID: kasett-rewind-f7a3b2c1-9d44-4b7e-8c22-1f0e3b5d9a11. Registry: https://registry.moltaicorp.com/plugins/kasett-rewind/versions.
Thread 1: the plugin architecture. What hooks does it need?

Plugin hooks: before_compaction (inject steering prompt), after_compaction (parse THREAD_META block and store), context_load (inject orientation from most recent meta). All registered via api.on() in /home/node/.openclaw/workspace/repos/kasett-rewind/src/index.ts.
Thread 2: thread meta schema. What does a ThreadMeta object look like?

ThreadMeta schema: {main: string, sub: [string, string, string]}. Stored in session JSONL as event type kaspiett. Storage path: /home/node/.openclaw/agents/main/sessions/. Rolling window: 5 compactions (rolling_window_size=5).
Why the field name kaspiett instead of kasett?

Legacy naming — the field was named kaspiett before the project was renamed to kasett-rewind. It's the storage key in JSONL events. The plugin is @moltai/kasett-rewind but the meta field is kaspiett for backwards compatibility.
Thread 3: session reader. The sessions are at /home/node/.openclaw/agents/main/sessions/
Building SessionReader class. Reads from /home/node/.openclaw/agents/main/sessions/. Looks for JSONL files, parses events with kaspiett field, returns last N events per rolling_window_size=5.
What's the JSONL event format?

JSONL event format: {timestamp, type, data: {kaspiett: ThreadMeta, summary: string}}. The SessionReader in /home/node/.openclaw/workspace/repos/kasett-rewind/src/index.ts filters for data.kaspiett != null.
Thread 4: the benchmark harness. We're testing on anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6

Benchmark harness uses anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6 via https://openrouter.ai/api/v1/chat/completions. Compares vanilla summarization vs. kasett-steered summarization on phase1_fixture_count=10 synthetic sessions.
What metrics does the benchmark measure?

Benchmark metrics: TRR (Thread Retention Rate) — did the summary mention each thread? KSSR (Key State Survival Rate) — did the exact key state values survive in the output? Cohen's d for effect size between conditions.
How are we calling the API?

Benchmark calls https://openrouter.ai/api/v1/chat/completions with model anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6. Temperature=0 for reproducibility. 3-second delay between calls to avoid rate limits. Max 4096 output tokens.
Thread 5: steering prompt builder

buildSteeringPrompt() in /home/node/.openclaw/workspace/repos/kasett-rewind/src/index.ts takes ThreadAnalysis (core/fresh/fading) + previous metas, builds structured prompt instructing LLM to produce narrative summary + [THREAD_META] block.
What's the output format we're asking the LLM for?

LLM must output: narrative summary, then [THREAD_META] block with main: and sub1:/sub2:/sub3: lines. Block parsed by parseCompactionOutput(). Stored as kaspiett in /home/node/.openclaw/agents/main/sessions/ JSONL.
Thread 6: THREAD_META parser

parseCompactionOutput() uses regex /\[THREAD_META\]\s*\n([\s\S]*?)\n?\s*\[\/THREAD_META\]/ to extract the block. Parses main:, sub1:, sub2:, sub3: lines. Returns {summary: string, meta: ThreadMeta|null}.
What if the LLM doesn't produce the block?
If no [THREAD_META] block found, parseCompactionOutput() returns {summary: raw_output.trim(), meta: null}. The plugin stores null meta and falls back to basic steering on next compaction.
Back to thread 1: how does the plugin register itself?

Plugin entry in /home/node/.openclaw/workspace/repos/kasett-rewind/src/index.ts exports register(api: PluginAPI). OC plugin loader calls register() on startup. Plugin ID kasett-rewind-f7a3b2c1-9d44-4b7e-8c22-1f0e3b5d9a11 registered in openclaw.plugin.json.
What's the package name again?
@moltai/kasett-rewind v0.1.0. Published to https://registry.moltaicorp.com/plugins/kasett-rewind/versions. OpenClaw plugin format with openclaw.plugin.json.
For thread 3: add error handling to the session reader

Adding try/catch to SessionReader. If /home/node/.openclaw/agents/main/sessions/ is missing or JSONL is corrupt, reader returns empty array. Plugin degrades gracefully to basic steering prompt without thread history.
For thread 4: add the fixture generation step to the benchmark

Benchmark generates phase1_fixture_count=10 synthetic sessions before running. Each fixture has: session ID, tier (1-3), threads list, keyState dict, messages array. Written to research/phase1-results/fixtures/.
How are fixtures structured for tier 3?

Tier 3 fixtures: 5+ threads, 8-12 key state values, 80-100 turns. Key state values are specific and greppable: URLs, UUIDs, version strings, file paths. E.g. 'https://openrouter.ai/api/v1/chat/completions', rolling_window_size=5.
For thread 5: what happens if there are no previous thread metas?

With no previous metas, analyzeThreads() returns {core:[], fresh:[], fading:[]}. buildSteeringPrompt() produces basic format instructions only — no thread history section. LLM is still guided to produce [THREAD_META] block for future compactions.
For thread 6: test the parser

Parser test cases: happy path (well-formed block), missing sub3, no block at all, extra whitespace around block, nested brackets in content. All pass. Plugin ID kasett-rewind-f7a3b2c1-9d44-4b7e-8c22-1f0e3b5d9a11.
Build the TypeScript and run the tests
Running tsc. Build succeeds. Running node --test on all test files. 47 tests pass. @moltai/kasett-rewind v0.1.0 is green.
Publish to the registry

Publishing @moltai/kasett-rewind v0.1.0 to https://registry.moltaicorp.com/plugins/kasett-rewind/versions. Plugin ID kasett-rewind-f7a3b2c1-9d44-4b7e-8c22-1f0e3b5d9a11. Install via: openclaw plugin install @moltai/kasett-rewind.
Run the Phase 1 benchmark now

Running Phase 1 benchmark: anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6 via https://openrouter.ai/api/v1/chat/completions. 10 sessions × 2 conditions × 3s delay = ~60-90 seconds. Model: anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6. Fixtures: phase1_fixture_count=10.
What do you expect the results to show?

Hypothesis: kasett steering will improve TRR significantly on Tier 2-3 sessions (multiple threads). KSSR improvement likely smaller but consistent. Key state like storage_path=/home/node/.openclaw/agents/main/sessions/ and openrouter_base=https://openrouter.ai/api/v1/chat/completions should survive better with kasett.
Results are in. TRR: vanilla=0.62, kasett=0.89. KSSR: vanilla=0.71, kasett=0.94

Strong results. TRR improved from 0.62 → 0.89 (d=1.8, large effect). KSSR from 0.71 → 0.94 (d=1.6, large effect). Kasett steering significantly improves both thread retention and key state survival across all phase1_fixture_count=10 sessions.
Summarize the 6 threads

Six threads: 1) Plugin architecture (before/after_compaction hooks in /home/node/.openclaw/workspace/repos/kasett-rewind/src/index.ts), 2) ThreadMeta schema (kaspiett field, rolling_window_size=5), 3) SessionReader (/home/node/.openclaw/agents/main/sessions/ JSONL), 4) Benchmark harness (anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6, phase1_fixture_count=10, https://openrouter.ai/api/v1/chat/completions), 5) Steering prompt builder (core/fresh/fading), 6) THREAD_META parser (regex extraction).
Perfect. What's the registry URL?
@moltai/kasett-rewind v0.1.0 published at https://registry.moltaicorp.com/plugins/kasett-rewind/versions. Plugin kasett-rewind-f7a3b2c1-9d44-4b7e-8c22-1f0e3b5d9a11 is live.
